The **Scotland men's national cricket team**[[b]](#cite_note-7) represents the country of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in international cricket and is administered by [Cricket Scotland](/wiki/Cricket_Scotland "Cricket Scotland"). They compete in international competitions organised by the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), including the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") and the [ICC Men's T20 World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "ICC Men's T20 World Cup"). They play most of their home matches at [The Grange, Edinburgh](/wiki/The_Grange_Club "The Grange Club"), as well as at other venues around Scotland.

Scotland's history in cricket dates back to the 18th century, and during the 19th and 20th centuries they frequently played touring teams and counties. In 1992, the Scottish Cricket Union severed links with the [ECB](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") and in 1994 they became [Associate Members](/wiki/Associate_membership_of_the_ICC "Associate membership of the ICC") of the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC).[[6]](#cite_note-ScoCA-8) The Scottish Cricket Union changed its name to [Cricket Scotland](/wiki/Cricket_Scotland "Cricket Scotland") in 2001.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

Scotland have played in three [ODI World Cups](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") (1999, 2007 and 2015) and five [T20 World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "ICC Men's T20 World Cup") tournaments (2007, 2009, 2016, 2021 and 2022). However, their first win in either of these events did not come until they beat [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_cricket_team "Hong Kong national cricket team") in the [2016 T20 World Cup](/wiki/2016_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2016 ICC World Twenty20").[[8]](#cite_note-10) Scotland have played in every [ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup") tournament, winning the inaugural edition in [2004](/wiki/2004_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2004 ICC Intercontinental Cup"). In April 2018, the ICC decided to grant full [Twenty20 International](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") (T20I) status to all its members. Therefore, all [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") matches played between Scotland and other [ICC members](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members "List of International Cricket Council members") after 1 January 2019 are a full T20I.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Before ICC Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before ICC Membership")]

The first recorded cricket match in Scotland took place in [Alloa](/wiki/Alloa "Alloa") in 1785.[[10]](#cite_note-Hist-12) It would be another eighty years, however, before Scotland's national side played their first full match, against the English county [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey_County_Cricket_Club "Surrey County Cricket Club") in 1865, which they won by 172 runs.[[11]](#cite_note-13) The first Scottish Cricket Union was formed in 1879, and the national team beat [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") by 7 wickets three years later.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) The cricket union became defunct in 1883, and Grange Cricket Club took over the administration of the game until 1909. The first match against [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team") took place in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") in 1888, with Ireland winning.[[14]](#cite_note-16)

Scotland played their first match to be awarded [first-class status](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") against the touring Australians in 1905, with the Scottish side being captained to a draw by [Hubert Johnston](/wiki/Hubert_Johnston_(cricketer) "Hubert Johnston (cricketer)").[[15]](#cite_note-17) They also played South Africa, West Indies, an all-Indian team, and New Zealand before the start of World War II.[[16]](#cite_note-18) 1948 saw Australia visit Scotland for two games at the end of their tour of England. These games, both of which Australia won by an innings, were to be the last international games for [Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman"). The Don signed off in typical style, making a fine unbeaten 123 in the second match.[[17]](#cite_note-Ref_c-19) In 1954, Scotland hosted a three-match to the touring [Pakistanis](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team"), which they lost by 10 wickets.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Five years later, they held [India](/wiki/India_national_cricket_team "India national cricket team") to a draw.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

The Pakistanis returned to Scotland in 1971, winning in [Selkirk](/wiki/Selkirk,_Scottish_Borders "Selkirk, Scottish Borders") by 154 runs.[[20]](#cite_note-22) The following year, Scotland gave the touring Australia side a scare in a low-scoring match, before succumbing to a six-wicket defeat.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Scotland hosted New Zealand in 1978, losing by an innings and 157 runs.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

### English domestic cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: English domestic cricket")]

As well as hosting touring sides, Scotland frequently played English first-class counties in the post-war period, and took part in the [Benson & Hedges Cup](/wiki/1980_Benson_%26_Hedges_Cup "1980 Benson & Hedges Cup") for the first time in 1980.[[23]](#cite_note-25) After enduring several winless seasons, their first Benson & Hedges victory came against [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire_County_Cricket_Club "Lancashire County Cricket Club") in 1986.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Their second win came in 1990 when they beat [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire_County_Cricket_Club "Northamptonshire County Cricket Club"), a match in which a 20-year-old [Dougie Brown](/wiki/Dougie_Brown "Dougie Brown") took three wickets.[[25]](#cite_note-27) When the tournament was reduced to the top eight [County Championship](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship") teams for the 1999 season, they were unable to qualify and they did not rejoin the competition thereafter. In 1983, they took part in the [NatWest Trophy](/wiki/1983_NatWest_Trophy "1983 NatWest Trophy"). Their first win in the knockout competition came against [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club") in 1998. [Gavin Haynes](/wiki/Gavin_Haynes "Gavin Haynes") hit 74 and Man of the Match [Craig Wright](/wiki/Craig_Wright_(cricketer) "Craig Wright (cricketer)") took 5/23 as Scotland sealed a famous four-run victory.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

In [the following season](/wiki/1999_NatWest_Trophy "1999 NatWest Trophy"), they made it to the third round of an expanded version of the competition, beating [Nottinghamshire Cricket Board](/wiki/Nottinghamshire_Cricket_Board "Nottinghamshire Cricket Board") and [Dorset County Cricket Club](/wiki/Dorset_County_Cricket_Club "Dorset County Cricket Club") before losing to [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey_County_Cricket_Club "Surrey County Cricket Club") in the Third Round. When the tournament was rebranded the [Cheltenham & Gloucester Trophy](/wiki/Cheltenham_%26_Gloucester_Trophy "Cheltenham & Gloucester Trophy"), Scotland were unable to take part in the 2001 edition due to their appearance in the [2001 ICC Trophy](/wiki/2001_ICC_Trophy "2001 ICC Trophy"). However they returned in [2002](/wiki/2002_Cheltenham_%26_Gloucester_Trophy "2002 Cheltenham & Gloucester Trophy"), enjoying wins over [Middlesex Cricket Board](/wiki/Middlesex_Cricket_Board "Middlesex Cricket Board") and Dorset. In 2003, Scotland beat the [Lancashire Cricket Board](/wiki/Lancashire_Cricket_Board "Lancashire Cricket Board") before losing out to [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset_County_Cricket_Club "Somerset County Cricket Club"). Similarly, the following year they enjoyed success against minor county [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_County_Cricket_Club "Cumberland County Cricket Club") before losing to [Essex](/wiki/Essex_County_Cricket_Club "Essex County Cricket Club").[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In 2006 the tournament moved to a group stage, Scotland enjoyed a considerably better campaign, winning three games against first-class counties.[[28]](#cite_note-30) Between 2007 and 2009, in what was now named the [Friends Provident Trophy](/wiki/Friends_Provident_Trophy "Friends Provident Trophy"), Scotland won one game in each season. When the Friends Provident Trophy ended, Scotland joined the Clydesdale Bank 40, registering two victories in the [2010 edition](/wiki/2010_Clydesdale_Bank_40 "2010 Clydesdale Bank 40"). They won twice more in the [2011 season](/wiki/2011_Clydesdale_Bank_40 "2011 Clydesdale Bank 40"), when they adopted the name Scottish Saltires. However, in the [following season](/wiki/2012_Clydesdale_Bank_40 "2012 Clydesdale Bank 40") they recorded just one victory, and their final season, in [2013](/wiki/2013_Clydesdale_Bank_40 "2013 Clydesdale Bank 40"), was winless. Then the [Royal London Cup](/wiki/Royal_London_Cup "Royal London Cup") was launched in 2014, but no international teams were included. Since then, [Cricket Scotland](/wiki/Cricket_Scotland "Cricket Scotland") has expressed interest in playing in county cricket again, with the [T20 Blast](/wiki/T20_Blast "T20 Blast") said to be their priority, but the Royal London Cup and [The Hundred](/wiki/The_Hundred_(cricket) "The Hundred (cricket)") also of interest.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

### ICC Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: ICC Membership")]

In 1992, Scotland severed their ties with the [Test and County Cricket Board](/wiki/Test_and_County_Cricket_Board "Test and County Cricket Board") (TCCB) and England, and gained [Associate Membership of the ICC](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members#Associate_Members "List of International Cricket Council members") in their own right in 1994.[[6]](#cite_note-ScoCA-8) They competed in the [ICC Trophy](/wiki/ICC_World_Cup_Qualifier "ICC World Cup Qualifier") for the first time in [1997](/wiki/1997_ICC_Trophy "1997 ICC Trophy"), finishing third. They were denied a place in the final only by [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh_cricket_team "Bangladesh cricket team"), who were granted full ICC Membership the following year and Test Status in 2001. In the third-place playoff, they beat [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team") by 51 runs, sealing their qualification for the [1999 World Cup](/wiki/1999_Cricket_World_Cup "1999 Cricket World Cup"),

Although England were official hosts of the World Cup, Scotland earned the right to host two of their matches in [The Grange Club](/wiki/The_Grange_Club "The Grange Club") in Edinburgh.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

Scotland's first ODI was played against eventual champions Australia. While they lost the game, they were competitive and a very strong Australia team reached the target after almost 45 overs.[[32]](#cite_note-WC99-34)

The rest of the tournament featured some chastening defeats, however, especially to the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_cricket_team "New Zealand national cricket team"), who raced their way to low totals. In what some described as the team's "own Cup Final",[[33]](#cite_note-35) the team lost narrowly to Bangladesh. Nonetheless, several players went home with their reputations enhanced, including [Gavin Hamilton](/wiki/Gavin_Hamilton_(cricketer) "Gavin Hamilton (cricketer)"), who scored 217 runs at 54.25 and [John Blain](/wiki/John_Blain_(cricketer) "John Blain (cricketer)"), who took 10 wickets at 21.00.

The [2001 ICC Trophy](/wiki/2001_ICC_Trophy "2001 ICC Trophy") saw them finish 4th, losing a play-off game to [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_cricket_team "Canada national cricket team"),[[34]](#cite_note-ICCT01-36) but they won the [2005 tournament](/wiki/2005_ICC_Trophy "2005 ICC Trophy"), beating long-time rivals Ireland in the final. In 2004, Scotland further staked their claim as one of the leading associate nations by winning the inaugural [Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "ICC Intercontinental Cup"). However, they did not progress beyond the first round in the 2005 tournament.[[35]](#cite_note-ICCup05-37)

### 2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2006")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Ryan_watson_india_odi.jpg/250px-Ryan_watson_india_odi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ryan_watson_india_odi.jpg)

Scotland's [Ryan Watson](/wiki/Ryan_Watson_(cricketer) "Ryan Watson (cricketer)") plays through backward point for a boundary against India at Glasgow's [Titwood](/wiki/Citylets_Titwood "Citylets Titwood") ground, 16 August 2007

March 2006 saw Scotland embark on a pre-season tour to [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"). They performed with some credit, although they only won one of their 6 games, against a Barbados XI.[[36]](#cite_note-Ref_d-38) They owed much of their success to Nik Morton, who re-qualified to represent Scotland internationally in 2004. They competed in the [C & G Trophy](/wiki/Friends_Provident_Trophy "Friends Provident Trophy") in [English domestic cricket](/wiki/County_cricket "County cricket") in the early part of the [2006 English cricket season](/wiki/2006_English_cricket_season "2006 English cricket season"). They performed better than expected, winning three of their nine games, and finishing eighth in the Northern conference.[[37]](#cite_note-Ref_e-39)

In June, they played their first ODI since the [1999 World Cup](/wiki/1999_Cricket_World_Cup "1999 Cricket World Cup") when they took on [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team") in Edinburgh.[[38]](#cite_note-ODIList-40) Without key players Dougie Brown and [Navdeep Poonia](/wiki/Navdeep_Poonia "Navdeep Poonia"), they lost by five wickets.[[39]](#cite_note-Ref_f-41) They finally got their first ODI win in the [European Championships](/wiki/European_Cricket_Championship "European Cricket Championship") in August with a win over [Holland](/wiki/Netherlands_national_cricket_team "Netherlands national cricket team") in a rain-shortened game.[[40]](#cite_note-Ref_g-42) They again missed key players for some games in this tournament though, and thanks to their loss against Ireland, finished second in the tournament.[[41]](#cite_note-EC06-43)

During 2006 and early 2007, Scotland participated in the third edition of the [Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2006–07 ICC Intercontinental Cup"). They beat [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia_national_cricket_team "Namibia national cricket team") by an innings in May 2006, but draws against Ireland in August and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_cricket_team "United Arab Emirates national cricket team") in January 2007 meant that they failed to reach the final.[[42]](#cite_note-ICCup06-44) In December 2006, they travelled to Test nation [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") for a two-match ODI series – their first outside the UK – but lost both matches heavily.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

### 2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2007")]

In January 2007, after the Intercontinental Cup match against United Arab Emirates in [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah_(city) "Sharjah (city)"), they travelled to [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), first playing in a [tri-series](/wiki/Associates_Triangular_Series_in_Kenya_in_2006-07 "Associates Triangular Series in Kenya in 2006-07") against [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_cricket_team "Canada national cricket team") and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_national_cricket_team "Kenya national cricket team") in [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa"), where they finished second.[[44]](#cite_note-Ref_h-46) This was followed by [Division One](/wiki/2007_ICC_World_Cricket_League_Division_One "2007 ICC World Cricket League Division One") of the [World Cricket League](/wiki/World_Cricket_League "World Cricket League") in [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), where Scotland finished as runners up.[[45]](#cite_note-WCL107-47)

They then travelled to [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") for their second [World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup"). They again lost all their games and failed to progress beyond the first round.[[46]](#cite_note-WC07-48) Back in the UK, they competed in the [Friends Provident Trophy](/wiki/Friends_Provident_Trophy "Friends Provident Trophy"), their only win coming against Lancashire.[[47]](#cite_note-Ref_i-49) They also drew an Intercontinental Cup match against United Arab Emirates.[[48]](#cite_note-Ref_j-50) An ODI against Pakistan in July was washed out.[[49]](#cite_note-Ref_k-51)

In July, Scotland took part in a [quadrangular series](/wiki/Quadrangular_Series_in_Ireland_in_2007 "Quadrangular Series in Ireland in 2007") in Ireland against the hosts, Holland and [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team"). However, the endeavour was not a success. They lost their matches against Ireland and West Indies, with the match against Holland being abandoned due to rain.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

At the beginning of August, Scotland were on Intercontinental Cup duty as they beat Holland by an innings and 59 runs. They then drew with Ireland in a rain-affected match, only gaining 3 points after a poor 1st innings display. [India](/wiki/India_national_cricket_team "India national cricket team") were Scotland's next ODI opponents in mid-August, which was shown live on BBC Scotland from Titwood, Glasgow. The match was reduced slightly to 46 overs after a couple of brief showers, but India won by 7 wickets.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

Having reached the final of the [World Cricket League](/wiki/World_Cricket_League "World Cricket League") earlier in the year, Scotland qualified to play in the [Twenty20 World Championship](/wiki/2007_Twenty20_World_Championship "2007 Twenty20 World Championship") held in South Africa. They lost by 51 runs to Pakistan in their first game, and did not get a chance to play their other Group D opponents [India](/wiki/India_national_cricket_team "India national cricket team"), as the game was washed out without a ball being bowled.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55)

### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2008")]

In July 2008, Scotland played a [tri-series](/wiki/2008_Associates_Tri-Series_in_Scotland "2008 Associates Tri-Series in Scotland") against New Zealand and Ireland in [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), Scotland. Scotland beat Ireland but lost their match against New Zealand.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

In early August, Scotland participated with five other Associate nations in the [2009 ICC World Twenty20 Qualifier](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20_Qualifier "2009 ICC World Twenty20 Qualifier") in Belfast. Despite an initial loss to hosts Ireland, victory against Bermuda secured a semi final slot. Throwing off the disappointment of an unexpected loss to Holland in the semi-final a few hours earlier, Scotland bounced right back for a 9 wicket victory over Kenya (who had advanced ahead of Canada), to secure third place. However, with only two nations guaranteed to progress, qualification for the [2009 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2009 ICC World Twenty20") was only granted when Zimbabwe confirmed that they would not attend the tournament.

On 18 August, Scotland played their first ODI encounter against England. Hosting the Auld Enemy, at the Grange Cricket Club in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"). However the match was abandoned due to rain after less than 3 overs of England's reply to Scotland's 156/9.

In December 2008, [Cricket Scotland](/wiki/Cricket_Scotland "Cricket Scotland"), the governing body of Scottish cricket, took the historic act of giving three Scotland players central contracts. Bowlers [Gordon Goudie](/wiki/Gordon_Goudie "Gordon Goudie") and [Dewald Nel](/wiki/Dewald_Nel "Dewald Nel") and captain [Ryan Watson](/wiki/Ryan_Watson_(cricketer) "Ryan Watson (cricketer)") became the first full-time professional cricketers based in Scotland. Nineteen other cricketers have been offered part-time professional deals.[[55]](#cite_note-Ref_2008-57)

### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2009")]

Scotland participated in the [2009 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2009 ICC World Twenty20") in England in June 2009. They were drawn alongside Test nations [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_cricket_team "New Zealand national cricket team") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_cricket_team "South Africa national cricket team") in Group D, with both matches being played at [The Oval](/wiki/The_Oval "The Oval") in London.

The first match, against New Zealand, was shortened to 7 overs per side due to rain. Scotland batted first and made 89/4, with [Kyle Coetzer](/wiki/Kyle_Coetzer "Kyle Coetzer") top-scoring with 33. However, three no-balls and a dropped catch enabled New Zealand to win by seven wickets with an over to spare.[[56]](#cite_note-Ref_2009-58)

In the second match, South Africa made 211/5, with [AB de Villiers](/wiki/AB_de_Villiers "AB de Villiers") hitting 79 not out off only 34 balls. In response, Scotland were bowled out for 81, more than half of which was scored by Coetzer (42). The 130-run margin of defeat was the second-largest in terms of runs in a Twenty20 International.[[57]](#cite_note-Ref_2009a-59)

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010")]

In 2010, Scotland took part in the inaugural [ECB 40](/wiki/ECB_40 "ECB 40") tournament.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

Scotland competed in the qualifiers in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), to compete for a place in the [2010 ICC World Twenty20](/wiki/2010_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2010 ICC World Twenty20") in the [West Indies](/wiki/British_West_Indies "British West Indies"). They competed for a place with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_national_cricket_team "Afghanistan national cricket team"), [Canada](/wiki/Canadian_national_cricket_team "Canadian national cricket team"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_cricket_team "Ireland national cricket team"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya_national_cricket_team "Kenya national cricket team"), [Holland](/wiki/Netherlands_national_cricket_team "Netherlands national cricket team"), [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_cricket_team "United Arab Emirates national cricket team") and [USA](/wiki/United_States_national_cricket_team "United States national cricket team").[[59]](#cite_note-Ref_l-61) The tournament was disappointing for Scotland, going out in the group stage without winning a single match.

Scotland's Intercontinental Cup campaign was more successful as they reached the final in December – against Afghanistan – at the bespoke new cricket stadium in Dubai. Scheduled as a four-day first-class match of two innings each side, Afghanistan won the game in eight sessions. The match was live-streamed online by Cricket Scotland in agreement with the ICC.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### ICC World Cup Qualifiers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: ICC World Cup Qualifiers")]

During March and April 2009 Scotland attempted to defend the ICC Trophy they won in 2005. To secure qualification for the [2011 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2011_Cricket_World_Cup "2011 Cricket World Cup") a top four place was targeted. They were also attempting to secure ODI status by finishing in the top six.

Scotland started the tournament badly by losing three of their five group games. With only the points earned against Namibia being taken through to the Super Eights, Scotland faced a difficult route to the World Cup.[[61]](#cite_note-Ref_2009b-63)

Scotland started the Super Eights well by beating Holland in their first match. Defeats against Kenya and Afghanistan followed. The result of which threatened Scotland's qualification for the World Cup as well as the possibility of losing their ODI status if they finished out of the top six.

Victory against United Arab Emirates in their last game, and an improved run-rate, thanks to the 122 run victory, ensured a top six place for the Scots, securing ODI status until the next round of World Cup qualifiers.

In 2012, Scotland achieved their first victory against a [full member](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members "List of International Cricket Council members") of the [ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") when it defeated a [touring](/wiki/Bangladeshi_cricket_team_in_Ireland_and_Netherlands_in_2012 "Bangladeshi cricket team in Ireland and Netherlands in 2012") [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_cricket_team "Bangladesh national cricket team") side.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

Locations of all stadiums which have hosted an international cricket match within Scotland

Scotland currently has five grounds which have hosted internationals.

[The Grange Club](/wiki/The_Grange_Club "The Grange Club") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"). The club itself was founded in 1832, and the current location has been in operation since 1872. The ground seats 5,000 people and has hosted 22 ODI's and 27 T20I's, including two matches from the 1999 Cricket World Cup.[[76]](#cite_note-espncricinfo.com-78)

[Titwood](/wiki/Titwood "Titwood") in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"). [Clydesdale Cricket Club](/wiki/Clydesdale_Cricket_Club "Clydesdale Cricket Club"), which was founded in 1848, moved here in 1876. Titwood hosted its first first-class match against [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") in July 1963. Between 1984 and 1986 the ground hosted an annual fixture against [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team"). It has hosted six ODI's, the most recent being in 2022.[[76]](#cite_note-espncricinfo.com-78)

[Cambusdoon New Ground](/wiki/Cambusdoon_New_Ground "Cambusdoon New Ground") in [Ayr](/wiki/Ayr "Ayr"). In 2006, Cambusdoon was approved by the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC) to host ODI matches. The ground hosted Scotland's [2015–17 ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9317_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2015–17 ICC Intercontinental Cup") matches between [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_cricket_team "United Arab Emirates national cricket team") and Namibia.

[Mannofield Park](/wiki/Mannofield_Park "Mannofield Park") in Aberdeen. Mannofield was also approved for ODI matches in 2006. The ground's history dates back to 1879 when [Aberdeen University](/wiki/Aberdeen_University "Aberdeen University") played host to [Edinburgh University](/wiki/Edinburgh_University "Edinburgh University"). The ground also welcomed [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_cricket_team "Ireland national cricket team") in 1930 and was the stadium where Sir Donald Bradman, who scored his last century on British soil at the ground in 1948. It has hosted 22 ODI's and 2 T20I's. The ground has played host to 11 first-class matches, the last of which came in July 2013 when Scotland beat Kenya in the [2011–2013 ICC Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2011%E2%80%932013_ICC_Intercontinental_Cup "2011–2013 ICC Intercontinental Cup").

[Forthill](/wiki/Forthill "Forthill") in [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee"). Forthill is the home of [Forfarshire Cricket Club](/wiki/Forfarshire_Cricket_Club "Forfarshire Cricket Club") and was opened in 1880 with a match between Forfarshire and [Glenalmond College](/wiki/Glenalmond_College "Glenalmond College"). In the 20th century several first-class matches took place there, including games against touring Indians and New Zealanders. It hosted its first ODI's in 2024, playing host to Namibia and Oman in the [ICC Men's Cricket World Cup League 2](/wiki/2024%E2%80%932026_ICC_Cricket_World_Cup_League_2 "2024–2026 ICC Cricket World Cup League 2").[[77]](#cite_note-79)

## Notable Scottish cricketers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scotland_national_cricket_team&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notable Scottish cricketers")]

Many famous cricketers have hailed from Scotland, most notably former England captain, [Mike Denness](/wiki/Mike_Denness "Mike Denness"), who was born in [Lanarkshire](/wiki/Lanarkshire "Lanarkshire") and represented Scotland both before and during his time at [Kent.](/wiki/Kent_County_Cricket_Club "Kent County Cricket Club")

[Douglas Jardine](/wiki/Douglas_Jardine "Douglas Jardine"), a particularly infamous cricketer, and a man who was vilified in Australia, was born in [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") to Scottish parents. He developed [leg theory](/wiki/Leg_theory "Leg theory"), which led to the notorious "[Bodyline](/wiki/Bodyline "Bodyline")" Ashes series in [1932-33](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_Australia_in_1932%E2%80%9333 "English cricket team in Australia in 1932–33"). Despite living most of his life in England, Jardine gave his children Scottish names and asked for his ashes to be scattered in Scotland.

Arguably one of Scotland's best spinners - and also a respected journalist - was [Ian Peebles](/wiki/Ian_Peebles "Ian Peebles"),[[78]](#cite_note-80) who was one of the [Wisden Cricketers of the Year](/wiki/Wisden_Cricketers_of_the_Year "Wisden Cricketers of the Year") in 1931, alongside [Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman").

Another Scottish spinner was [Peter Such](/wiki/Peter_Such "Peter Such"), who took 849 first-class wickets and made 11 test appearances for England. In a 1999 test against New Zealand, Such broke the world-record for the longest duck, surviving 51 balls and allowing England to add 31 for the ninth wicket in a scrappy draw.

[Brian Hardie](/wiki/Brian_Hardie "Brian Hardie") was a major contributor to the Essex side of the 1970s and 1980s, which won four [County Championships](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship"), three Sunday Leagues, a [Benson & Hedges Cup](/wiki/Benson_%26_Hedges_Cup "Benson & Hedges Cup") and a [NatWest Trophy](/wiki/1985_NatWest_Trophy "1985 NatWest Trophy"), in which he was Man of the Match after scoring 110 in a one-run victory.. The regular opening partner of [Graham Gooch](/wiki/Graham_Gooch "Graham Gooch"), Hardie retired with more than 18,000 first class runs.

Stirling-born all-rounder [Dougie Brown](/wiki/Dougie_Brown "Dougie Brown") enjoyed a fifteen-year career with Warwickshire and represented both England and Scotland.

The same was the case for all-rounder [Gavin Hamilton](/wiki/Gavin_Hamilton_(cricketer) "Gavin Hamilton (cricketer)"), who played for both Yorkshire and Durham in domestic cricket, and starred in the 1999 World Cup for Scotland, before playing a test for England in the same year.
